coredata.dc:title: Mineralization and morphology control of CaCO3 induced by glutamic acid derivatized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Lina
Chen, Xia
Zhang, Guolin
Yang, Wenxiao
Wu, Qiuhua
coredata.dc:description: Calcium carbonate (CaCO3) exhibits multiple morphologies and properties to meet different requirements through biomineralization in nature. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by a microwave method using glutamic acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials. The mineralization of CaCO3 was subsequently proceeded in the presence of CDs by means of gas diffusion. The effects of the ratio of glutamic acid to ethylenediamine, the concentrations of CDs and Ca2+ on the mineralization process were studied. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer, X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscope (SEM), and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were employed for characterizations of CDs and CaCO3. The results indicated that the CDs had a uniform size of 4–6 nm and good dispersion in the aqueous phase due to the numerous –COOH groups on the surface. The crystallization of CaCO3 was strongly affected by CDs, presenting various morphologies of rhombic, spherical, hexagonal, hydrangea-shaped, and cobblestone-shaped. The presence of CDs was conducive to the formation and stable existence of vaterite and the content of vaterite was up to 97.61%. The possible mechanism proposed was related to the –COOH groups on CDs. The multiple controllable forms of CaCO3 may provide various applications such as drug sustained release and so on.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B1. Carbon dots
B1. Calcium carbonate
A1. Morphology control

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mineralization of CaCO3 induced by CDs
Effects of CDs on the crystallization of CaCO3
Effect of Ca2+ concentration on the crystallization of CaCO3
Possible mechanism of mineralization process of CaCO3 induced by CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51373073 ), and the Doctor Start-up Fund of Liaoning University (No. a280008030 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of CaCO3 crystals induced by various concentrations of CDs-1 (g/L) (a: 0.1, b: 0.5, c: 1.0, d: 2.0; [Ca2+] = 20 mmol/L).
XRD patterns (a) and FTIR spectra (b) of CaCO3 crystals induced by various concentrations of CDs-1 ([Ca2+] = 20 mmol/L).
SEM images of CaCO3 crystals under different concentrations of Ca2+ (mmol/L) (a, b: 10; c, d: 20; e, f: 30; g, h: 40; i, j: 50; [CDs-1] = 2.0 g/L).
XRD patterns (a) and FTIR spectra (b) of CaCO3 crystals at various Ca2+ concentrations ([CDs-1] = 2.0 g/L).
SEM images of samples under different mineralization times (min) (a: 30, b: 60, c: 90, d: 120, e: 150; [Ca2+] = 20 mmol/L; [CDs-1] = 2.0 g/L).
Conductivities of CDs-CaCl2 hybrid solutions ([Ca2+] = 20 mmol/L).
Fluorescence emission spectra of dispersions of CDs-1 (a) and CaCO3 crystals (b).
Possible mechanism for vaterite mineralization process.

head.ce:title: Mineralization and morphology control of CaCO3 induced by glutamic acid derivatized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Calcium carbonate (CaCO3) exhibits multiple morphologies and properties to meet different requirements through biomineralization in nature. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by a microwave method using glutamic acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials. The mineralization of CaCO3 was subsequently proceeded in the presence of CDs by means of gas diffusion. The effects of the ratio of glutamic acid to ethylenediamine, the concentrations of CDs and Ca2+ on the mineralization process were studied. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer, X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscope (SEM), and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were employed for characterizations of CDs and CaCO3. The results indicated that the CDs had a uniform size of 4–6 nm and good dispersion in the aqueous phase due to the numerous –COOH groups on the surface. The crystallization of CaCO3 was strongly affected by CDs, presenting various morphologies of rhombic, spherical, hexagonal, hydrangea-shaped, and cobblestone-shaped. The presence of CDs was conducive to the formation and stable existence of vaterite and the content of vaterite was up to 97.61%. The possible mechanism proposed was related to the –COOH groups on CDs. The multiple controllable forms of CaCO3 may provide various applications such as drug sustained release and so on.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The biomineralization process plays an important role in nature, by which the structure, polymorphism, chemical composition, and morphology of biologically functional minerals can be carefully designed and precisely controlled. Inspired by nature’s masterpieces, biomimetic methods have been developed to produce inorganic minerals with complex structures and a variety of properties under environmental conditions.[1].

Carbonate mineral, is a kind of mineral widely found in the earth’s crust and applied on industrial scales, such as filler in paints, pigments, paper, and plastics.[2–4] Moreover, calcium carbonate (CaCO3), is an essential component in living organisms, such as bones, teeth, and shells.[5] For these reasons, CaCO3 has become one of the most critical subjects in mineralization.[6] Seven different forms of CaCO3 have been reported so far: calcite, vaterite, aragonite, monohydrocalcite (MHC) (CaCO3·1H2O), ikaite (CaCO3·6H2O), calcium carbonate hemihydrate (CCHH) (CaCO3·1/2H2O), and amorphous calcium carbonate (ACC).[7,8] Increasing evidence demonstrated that the formation of CaCO3 polycrystal began with the initial nucleation of ACC, followed by nanoparticle growth.[6] As is known to all, the polymorphism and morphology of minerals, which are very sensitive to synthesis conditions, play an important part in the physicochemical properties and the relevant applications.[5,9,10] Different additives, such as metal ions,[11–14] amino acids,[7,15,16] surfactants,[17,18] small molecule acids,[19] proteins,[20,21] and polymer,[22–24] were proved to have effective control on morphology, polymorphism, and structure of minerals. The effect of additives counts on two aspects: one is to interact with Ca2+, and the other is to adsorb on the specific crystal planes to reduce the surface energy.[25] There are usually functional groups on the surface of polymer additives, such as carboxyl and sulfonic acid groups, which may dissociate in water to give anions, and interact with Ca2+.[26,27] So that Ca2+ can be fixed in a small range by polymers with such groups via electrostatic force, resulting in a rise in the local concentration and the subsequent formation of crystal nuclei.[13,25] In some cases, calcite appeared when carbonyl groups rather than carboxyl groups presented in polymer’s chain.[28,29] In Neira-Carrillo’s study, sulfonated polymethylsiloxane (S-PMS) was used as template, and the crystal was still calcite.[30] When amylopectin was used as inducer, both calcite and vaterite emerged, and the maximum content for vaterite was 75%.[31].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new form of carbon analogs with a diameter of less than 10 nm and have extensive uses because of the simple preparation and easy surface modification.[32–34] Due to the complex oxidization during the preparation process of CDs, there are generally many functional groups on their surface, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and sometimes amino groups.[35–37] These polar groups enable CDs of good solubility in the aqueous phase and a high charge density on the surface.[38,39] If CDs are adopted into CaCO3 crystallization, the local concentration of Ca2+ will also be high, just as polymer inducers.[40,41] From this point of view, CDs may play the same role as polymer inducers in CaCO3 mineralization. Besides, CDs are spherical, and the shapes remain unchanged, while polymers in solution are in the form of random coils and their conformations vary from time to time. It is of great significance to study the role of CDs in CaCO3 mineralization. In our previous study, some uncommon morphologies of crystals have been observed during mineralization in the presence of CDs, but the crystals were only calcites.[42] The same situation happened in Gu’s and Rezwan’s research.[1,43] Other references mainly focused on the fluorescence and phosphorescence properties of CDs-inorganic compounds, rather than the morphology.[44,45] In addition, CDs may also affect the self-assembly behavior of some metal nanoparticles.[46].

Amino acids and hydrothermal method are common precursors and method for CDs’ synthesis, but the reaction time usually takes long. Generally, hetero atoms (N and S) doped in CDs, especially N could effectively improve the fluorescence emission.[37] Hence, ethylenediamine was selected as nitrogen source on the basis of using glutamic acid as carbon source to prepare CDs with fluorescent properties for easily detection in this study, and microwave method was adopted to shorten the reaction time.

In this paper, a series of CDs, which were prepared by glutamic acid and ethylenediamine at different feed ratios, were used as inducers in the mineralization of CaCO3. The effects of concentrations of CDs and Ca2+ on the crystallization were studied. CaCO3 crystals with various morphologies of rhombic, spherical, hexagonal, cobblestone-like, and hydrangea-like shapes were obtained correspondingly. The particles exhibited roughness discrepancies at different Ca2+ concentrations. The proportion of vaterite in CaCO3 synthesized in this paper was high, which could reach 97.61% at most, was different from the previous studies. Furthermore, a time gradient experiment was designed to monitor the growth process of CaCO3, and the possible formation mechanism was discussed.

All chemicals used in this paper were analytical grade, with no further refinement. Ammonium carbonate ((NH4)2CO3) and anhydrous calcium chloride (CaCl2) were purchased from Sinophenol Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. Glutamic acid was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine was purchased from Tianjin Bohua Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The ultrapure water was prepared in the lab, and the dialysis bag (500 MWCO) was purchased from Yibo Biological Technology Co., Ltd.

A typical microwave method was adopted to prepare the CDs. A certain amount of glutamic acid and ethylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, followed by 3 mins’ irradiation in a microwave oven with the power of 700 W, and the CDs paste was prepared. The CDs paste were then dispersed in 10 mL ultrapure water after cooled down to room temperature and transferred into a dialysis bag which was immersed in 150 mL ultrapure water for 24 h under stirring to remove the excess precursors and small molecules. Finally, the designed CDs were obtained after freeze-drying for 48 h at −48 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mineralization of CaCO3 induced by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CaCO3 particles were mineralized in the presence of CDs through gas diffusion method. In a typical synthesis, 5 mL of CaCl2 aqueous solution and specified amounts of CDs were put in a small beaker, of which a piece of conductive glass of 5 mm2 was placed in the bottom with the conductive surface up for CaCO3 collecting. The beaker and another beaker of fresh (NH4)2CO3 powder were covered with parafilm punched with four needle holes respectively and placed in the same closed system (a 1000 mL beaker with parafilm covered). A third beaker filled with 2/3 vol of concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was needed in the system to absorb the NH3 gas in the mineralization process and keep pH stable. The system was placed at 25 °C for 72 h. After the reaction, the conductive glass was carefully taken out, rinsed with ethanol, and naturally dried for morphology observation. XRD and FTIR were measured by using the CaCO3 particles attached to the walls of the beaker, which were carefully collected and dried as well.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images and particle sizes for CaCO3 were observed on a SU 8010 microscope (Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. The CaCO3, together with the conductive glass, was glued to a metal scanning table with conductive adhesive, sprayed with gold before determination. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a D8 Advance X-ray powder diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) under Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.542 Å), with a scanning rate of 4°/min, and the scan range of 2θ = 20°-65°. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was carried out on a Prestige-21 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) in the region 400–4000 cm-1. For FTIR analysis, the dry powder of CDs or CaCO3 was dispersed in KBr (2% wt/wt) and pressed as a 13 mm disc with minimum pressure. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images for CDs were obtained on a JEM-2100 microscope (Japan Electron Company) at an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. The samples were fabricated by transferring a suspension drop onto a 200-mesh copper grid and allowing the sample to dry in the air before measurement. A Leici DDS-307 conductometer (Shanghai Precision Instrument Ltd.) was employed to investigate the conductivity of the solution. Fluorescence measurements were performed on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

CDs can be obtained by partial dehydration and dehydrogenation of organics. The process involves multi-step organic reactions and various intermediates. Here, CDs were synthesized by using glutamic acid and ethylenediamine as the carbon donor and the passivator, respectively (Table S1).

TEM images (Figure S1) demonstrated that CDs had good dispersion in the aqueous phase, and the particles were mainly spheres with a diameter of 4.0–6.0 nm. FTIR spectra for CDs are shown in Figure S2a. The peak at 3422 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH.[47] The peaks at 1110, 1355, and 1606 cm−1 represented C-O bending, C–H and C = O tensile vibration modes, respectively.[48,49] It can be seen that the intensities of peaks of C = O at 1606 cm−1 and –OH at 3422 cm−1 of CDs-1 were slightly stronger than those of CDs-2 and CDs-3. The peak at 2881 cm−1 was attributed to the vibration of the amino and ammonium ions.[37,50] The surface of CDs was rich in carboxyl and amino groups, and maybe hydroxyl,[33] and there were more functional groups on the surface of CDs-1 than that of CDs-2 and CDs-3 from FTIR. The XRD pattern of CDs-1 is listed in Figure S2b. A relatively broad diffraction peak could be seen at around 2θ = 23°, indicating the imperfect graphite-like structure.[33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs on the crystallization of CaCO3
ce:section.ce:para: Fixed the concentration of Ca2+ at 20 mmol/L, the influence of CDs-1 on the mineralization of CaCO3 was studied in the concentrations from 0.1 to 2.0 g/L. SEM images (Fig. 1 ) revealed that the obtained CaCO3 was a mixture of spheres and a small amount of rhombus at all concentrations we studied. The spheres, including round and hexagonal ones, were generally vaterite, and the rhombus was the typical appearance for calcite. The crystalline grain size increased slightly from 6 to 10 μm at the concentration of 0.1 g/L to 10–15 μm at 2.0 g/L. The surfaces of the sphere crystals were rough.

The XRD patterns (Fig. 2 a) indicated that the CaCO3 particles were all mixtures of vaterite (JCPDS #05–0586) and calcite (PDF #33–0268).[51] The characteristic peaks were marked by C for calcite and V for vaterite in Fig. 2a, respectively. The proportions for vaterite (
f v
) and calcite (
f c
) for the mineralized samples were calculated according to Equation (1):[31]
(1)
f v
=
7.691
I v
110
I c
104
+ 7.691
I v
110
;
f c
= 1 -
f v

where
I v
110
and
I c
104
were the diffraction intensities of {110} crystal plane of vaterite and {104} crystal plane of calcite, respectively. The results illuminated that, with increasing the CDs concentration from 0.1 g/L to 2.0 g/L, the
f v
risen along from 66.38% to 89.48% (Fig. 2a). Increasing the CDs concentration was beneficial to the formation of vaterite.

The FTIR spectra are displayed in Fig. 2b. The peaks at 745 cm−1 and 1087 cm−1, were the characteristic peaks of vaterite.[4,52] The peaks at 712 cm−1 and 1422 cm−1, were the characteristic peaks of calcite.[52,53] The results also confirmed that the crystals were mixtures of vaterite and calcite.
Calcite only was obtained in the presence of CDs-2 or CDs-3 according to the SEM (Figure S3) and XRD (Figure S4) results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Ca2+ concentration on the crystallization of CaCO3
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of Ca2+ concentration on the mineralization process was studied in the presence of 2.0 g/L CDs-1. When the concentration of Ca2+ was 10 mmol/L, CaCO3 was a mixture of calcite and vaterite with rhombus, sphere, and pumpkin shapes (Fig. 3 a, b). Increasing the Ca2+ concentration to 20 and 30 mmol/L, more sphere particles and fewer rhombic ones appeared, the particles mainly presented cobblestone-shaped, and the roughness didn’t change obviously (Fig. 3c-f). The hydrangea-like shape of CaCO3 emerged at 40 mmol/L of Ca2+ (Fig. 3g), which had a rougher surface, as shown in the enlarged image (Fig. 3h). The vaterite particles showed protuberant edges under 50 mmol/L of Ca2+, and some particles presented defective spheres with slightly sunken (Fig. 3i, j).

The XRD (Fig. 4 a) and FTIR (Fig. 4b) results demonstrated that CaCO3 obtained at different Ca2+ concentrations were all mixtures of vaterite and calcite, and the fraction of vaterite increased as the concentration of Ca2+.

The crystallization process of CaCO3 can be strongly affected by mineralization time. The morphology evolution of CaCO3 on time was investigated, and the corresponding SEM images of the products are summarized in Fig. 5 .

After 30 min of mineralization, the irregularly shaped traces in the view were considered the ACC or the residue after volatilization, which attributed to the increase of the local Ca2+ concentration induced by CDs (Fig. 5a). As the reaction time reached 60 min, the traces became narrower and more circle-shaped, and mineral grains with a diameter of about 500 nm appeared in the center of the marks, indicating that ACC was forming a more regular and compact structure (Fig. 5b). The grains were assumed ACC due to the irregular appearance.[28] CDs-1 in the solution could help stabilize the colloidal ACC precursor and hold the concentrated solution on a small scale, which may favor nucleation. The local concentrated solution gradually condensed to form small particles at this stage. With increasing mineralization time, larger and structured grains emerged, mainly in sphere particles, with the traces getting further narrowed (Fig. 5c, d). When the reaction time reached 150 min, sphere and rhombus can be clearly recognized with well-developed shapes (Fig. 5e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of mineralization process of CaCO3 induced by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The supersaturation of the solution was relatively low when the gas diffusion method was utilized for mineralization, so polymer additives were usually employed to raise the supersaturation effectively in some research.[40,41] The CDs may have similar effects to carboxy-containing polymers in mineralization due to their spherical appearance and multiple carboxyl groups.

To clarify the mechanism of the mineralization process in the presence of CDs, the conductivities of CaCl2 solutions (20 mmol/L) with different concentrations of CDs-1 were determined (Fig. 6 ). The conductivity of the solution decreased sharply when the concentration of CDs-1 risen from 0 to 0.1 g/L. Continue to increase the concentration, and the conductivity decreased slowly. The CDs-1 exhibited an affinity for Ca2+ since there were lots of carboxylate groups on the surface. The adsorption of Ca2+ by CDs-1 made the local concentration of Ca2+ high, which was apt to the formation of crystal nuclei and propelled the crystal growth.[40].

A fluorescence spectrophotometer was employed to verify the residue of CDs in CaCO3. Upon irradiation with a UV lamp (372 nm), the suspension of CDs-1 emitted strong blue fluorescence at 426 nm (Fig. 7 a). While the emission maximum of the derived CaCO3 shifted to 446 nm (Fig. 7b) compared to that of free CDs-1 (426 nm). The red shift of CaCO3 (about 20 nm) may be due to the aggregation of CDs in CaCO3 crystals and the subsequent photon reabsorption and nonradiative energy transfer.[54] The results demonstrated that there was still a detected amount of CDs-1 residue in CaCO3 crystals. It can be concluded that the CDs-1 had great importance in the morpho synthesis of the crystals, and also, they can label the crystals for some cases.

The possible mechanism for mineralization of CaCO3 in the presence of CDs-1 was deduced. At the initial stage, Ca2+ in the solution were attracted by –COOH groups on the CDs surface (Fig. 8 a).[55] The diffusion of CO2 into the solution activated the formation of CaCO3 (Fig. 8b),[56] which was existed as tiny ACC particles and temporarily stabilized against crystallization by binding to CDs-1 (Fig. 8c). As more CO2 entered, the tiny ACC particles conglomerated into large ones with CDs-1 wrapped in (Fig. 8d). After that, the crystallization of CaCO3 occurred in the ACC particles, and crystal growth was accomplished by the removal of solvent and/or the binding additives and the formation of interparticle chemical bonds (Fig. 8e).[28,57] It is speculated that the growth of vaterite particles with final morphology may follow the spherulitic growth mechanism.[58] The high degree of supersaturation in the solution caused by CDs-1 resulted in continuous heterogeneous nucleation on the surface of primary crystals. Thus, spherical, pumpkin-like, cobblestone-like, and hydrangea-like particles with rough surfaces were obtained (Fig. 8f).

We believed that the high local concentration of Ca2+ was the critical factor in the formation of vaterite. Calcite was produced from the unbound Ca2+ ions since its formation has to be favored at low supersaturation.[59] The vaterite was evolved from ACC, which was temporarily stabilized around CDs, and the crystallization occurred in a high saturation state.[60] Due to the complexation of the CDs with Ca2+ in aqueous solutions, the saturated concentrations of Ca2+ in the presence of CDs was supposed to be higher than that in the absence of the additive. Thus, the relative content of vaterite (
f v
) increased along with CDs-1 concentration since more Ca2+ ions were bound (Fig. 2a). Nevertheless, even if the concentration of CDs-1 reached 2.0 g/L, there were always freely moving Ca2+ ions in the solution, which cannot be 100 % bound by CDs-1. The sharp decrease in conductivity and high
f v
(66.38%) indicated that most Ca2+ was confined when the concentration of CDs-1 was only 0.1 g/L since there were so many –COOH groups on the surface. Thus fixed the concentration of CDs-1 at 2.0 g/L, CDs-1 still had a strong attraction to Ca2+ as the concentrations of Ca2+ varied from 10 to 50 mmol/L. The
f v
increased as the concentrations of Ca2+ since the supersaturation also increased.

The residue of CDs in CaCO3 crystals was also crucial to the stable existence of vaterite during crystallization. Vaterite transforms easily and irreversibly into thermodynamically more stable forms when in contact with water via a solvent-mediated process,[23] if the Ca-O bonds of the CDs ligand are easily dissociated by water, the CDs are thought not to be occluded in the CaCO3 crystal. CDs in vaterite crystals had a strong binding effect on Ca2+, preventing the transformation of vaterite into more thermodynamically stable calcite.

The surface roughness of the crystal was related to the degree of supersaturation in solution. The higher supersaturation led to the faster nucleation and growth of crystals, rather than an orderly packing. Fixing the inducer concentration and increasing the Ca2+ concentration would raise the supersaturation of the solution, resulting in a coarser crystal (Fig. 3).

The amount of –COOH on the surface of CDs decreased as the content of ethylenediamine increased in CDs’ preparation, resulting in weak interaction between CDs and Ca2+ or ACC. Thus CDs-2 and CDs-3 revealed little regulation effect on the morphology.

CDs, which were synthesized from glutamic acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials, were introduced into the mineralization of CaCO3. A series of rhombic, spherical, pumpkin-like, cobblestone-liked, and hydrangea-like particles were correspondingly generated. The crystals were all mixtures of vaterite and calcite, and the fraction of vaterite increased along the concentration of CDs and Ca2+, respectively. Among them, the content of vaterite could be 97.61% at most. The detailed formation process of crystals was also discussed by changing mineralization time and concentration of Ca2+, which may provide some insights for understanding the mineralization process of CaCO3. This work has broadened our understanding of the mineralization mechanism and provided a reference for the development and application of CaCO3 with different morphologies.
Lina Wu: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xia Chen: Writing. Guolin Zhang: Investigation. Wenxiao Yang: Writing – review & editing. Qiuhua Wu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51373073), and the Doctor Start-up Fund of Liaoning University (No. a280008030).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcrysgro.2023.127330.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
